coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of a near-infrared excitation surface molecular imprinting ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensitive and rapid detecting perfluorooctane sulfonate in complex matrix
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Lingxi
Guo, Huiqin
Li, Jing
Yan, Liushui
Zhu, Enze
Liu, Xiaoming
Li, Kexin
coredata.dc:description: Construction of fluorescent probe for highly sensitive and selective detection of perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) in water and biological samples is a very important strategy in related pollutant monitoring and environmental health risk appraisal. To overcome the drawback of low sensitivity caused by high-back ground signal of the conventional sensor, a molecularly imprinted near-infrared excitation ratiometric fluorescent probe was constructed and employed to determine PFOS. The sensing process was achieved through the selectively recognition of specific cavities in the probe surface with analyte, accompanied by fluorescence quenching due to the photoinduced electron transfer effect between upconversion materials and PFOS. Under optimized experimental conditions, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the probe has good linearity against the concentrations of PFOS response divided into two segments within linear ranges of 0.001–0.1 nmol/L and 0.1–1 nmol/L, respectively, with low detection limit of 1 pmol/L. Selective experiment results indicate that the C-F chain length plays a dominant role in molecular recognition and high sensitively detection. The fabricated probe shows well detection performance in a wide pH range. Furthermore, real samples analyses indicate that such an efficient fluorescent probe has potentials in PFOS determination in surface water, human serum and egg extract sample analyses.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Upconversion
Molecular imprinting
Ratiometric fluorescence
PFOS
Biological sample

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurements of fluorescence response to PFOS
Fluorescence detection of PFOS by the UMIR probe
Selective detection of PFOS by the UMIR probe
Application to the real samples analysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21665018, 21966023 and 51568049); Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province of China (No. 20181BAB203014). We would like to appreciate Prof. Wen-Qing Tu from Research Institute of Poyang Lake, Jiangxi Academy of Science for the providing of Po-yang Lake water samples.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21665018 , 21966023 and 51568049 ); Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province of China (No. 20181BAB203014 ). We would like to appreciate Prof. Wen-Qing Tu from Research Institute of Poyang Lake, Jiangxi Academy of Science for the providing of Po-yang Lake water samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis routine and schematic illustration of the UMIR probe. (A) the synthesis process of the UCNPs-SiO2@MIPs and the B-UCNPs@SiO2, (B) the construction process of the UMIR probe.
FESEM image of (A) the pure SiO2, TEM images of (B) NaYF4:Yb3+, Er3+@NaGdF4 and (C) G-UCNPs-SiO2@MIPs. (D) The FT-IR spectrum of (a) APTES-modified G-UCNPs; (b) G-UCNPs-SiO2; (c) G-UCNPs-SiO2 @MIPs.

The influence in the sensitivity of the final content ratio (C/C, µg/mL:µg/mL) of the analysis identical material G-UCNPs-SiO2@MIPs and the analysis reference material B-UCNPs-SiO2 in the system. (A) the concentration ratio was 3:2, (B) the concentration ratio was 3:4, (C) the concentration ratio was 3:6. Error bars in the inset represent standard deviations for two parallel experiments.

The effect of (A) (a) pH values (3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0, and 9.0), (b) Zeta-potential of G-UCNPs-SiO2@MIPs dispersed at different pH medium; (B) NaCl concentration (0.01–0.5 M); (C) incubation time (5–60 min); (D) incubation temperature (15–60 ℃) on the UMIR probe fluorescence quenching efficiency at PFOS concentration of 0.4 nmol/L. The error bars were calculated from two parallel experiments, the concentration of the analysis identical material G-UCNPs-SiO2@MIPs and the analysis reference material B-UCNPs-SiO2 in the final system was 4.5 µg/mL and 6.0 µg/mL, respectively; the PFOS concentration was 0.4 nmol/L, standard deviations were below 5%.

Fluorescence emission spectra (λex = 980 nm) of ratiometric fluorescence probes upon exposure to different concentrations of PFOS (A) UMIR probe; (B) UNIR probe. CPFOS = 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1 nmol/L, respectively. Error bars in the inset represent standard deviations for two parallel experiments at pH = 4.0 (Tris-HCl buffer, 0.1 M).

Fluorescence emission spectra (λex =980 nm) of ratiometric fluorescence probes upon exposure to different concentrations of PFOS (A) UMIR probe; (B) UNIR probe. CPFOS = 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1 nmol/L, respectively. Error bars in the inset represent standard deviations for two parallel experiments at pH = 6.5 (Tris-HCl buffer, 0.1 M).

Fluorescence quenching efficiency of (A) UMIR probe and (B) UNIR probe by different kinds of substances at pH = 4.0 (Tris-HCl buffer, 0.1 M). Error bars represent standard deviations for two repeated experiments. (X axis represents the concentration of different substances, Y axis represents the sensitivity of different substances).
Comparison of recently reported fluorescent methods with our method for PFOS detection.
Fluorescence quenching constants and imprinting factor of developed UMIR probe.
Fluorescence quenching constants of UMIR probe and UNIR probe by different kinds of substances at pH = 4.0 (Tris-HCl buffer, 0.1 M) and chemical structure of the substance.
Recovery rate of PFOS spiked water samples and biological samples using the UMIR probe detection (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Fabrication of a near-infrared excitation surface molecular imprinting ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensitive and rapid detecting perfluorooctane sulfonate in complex matrix

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Jiangxi Province for Persistent Pollutants Control and Resources Recycle, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang HangKong University, Nanchang 330063, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Jiangxi Province for Persistent Pollutants Control and Resources Recycle, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang HangKong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Jiangxi Province for Persistent Pollutants Control and Resources Recycle, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang HangKong University, Nanchang 330063, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Construction of fluorescent probe for highly sensitive and selective detection of perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) in water and biological samples is a very important strategy in related pollutant monitoring and environmental health risk appraisal. To overcome the drawback of low sensitivity caused by high-back ground signal of the conventional sensor, a molecularly imprinted near-infrared excitation ratiometric fluorescent probe was constructed and employed to determine PFOS. The sensing process was achieved through the selectively recognition of specific cavities in the probe surface with analyte, accompanied by fluorescence quenching due to the photoinduced electron transfer effect between upconversion materials and PFOS. Under optimized experimental conditions, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the probe has good linearity against the concentrations of PFOS response divided into two segments within linear ranges of 0.001–0.1 nmol/L and 0.1–1 nmol/L, respectively, with low detection limit of 1 pmol/L. Selective experiment results indicate that the C-F chain length plays a dominant role in molecular recognition and high sensitively detection. The fabricated probe shows well detection performance in a wide pH range. Furthermore, real samples analyses indicate that such an efficient fluorescent probe has potentials in PFOS determination in surface water, human serum and egg extract sample analyses.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of C-8 series of perfluorinated compounds, perfluorooctane sulfonic acid (PFOS) is a synthetic organic fluorine chemical product developed by the 3 M company in 1952 (Zheng et al., 2019). PFOS is composed of hydrophilic sulfonic acid group and hydrophobic carbon chain with complete fluorination. This structure gives it unique physico-chemical properties that of chemical stability, amphiphilicity and high surface activity (Jin et al., 2020). PFOS and its derivative have been widespread employed to manufacture various commodities such as fine chemicals, firefighting foams, and waterproof agents (Wang et al., 2020). For example, as a typical fluorinated surfactant, the perfluorooctanesulfonyl fluoride (POSF) is used for water film foam extinguishing agent (AFFF) by the fire suppliers. In the firefighting operations, POSF enters the environment in a manner accompanied by the use of AFFF, and then is degraded to PFOS and induces environmental pollution (Leeson et al., 2020). It has reported that PFOS exists in almost all water bodies in various regions of the world (Guardian et al., 2020). Recently, it has been reported that the concentration of PFOS in the Yangtze estuary, Yellow sea, Bohai sea (Zhao et al., 2017) and the Great Lakes (Gewurtz et al., 2019) are located in the range of 0.03–0.2 ng/L and 2.1–7.4 ng/L, respectively. While, the regulation for acceptable PFOS concentration of 200 ng/L (Environmental Protection Agency EPA, 2014) and 0.65 ng/L (European Commission, Directive, 2013) for drinking and fresh water, respectively. Furthermore, Toxicological studies have confirmed that the existence of PFOS in tiny amounts would probably arouse serious functional damages to the human organs. Through the animal experiments of PFOS, researchers found that the intestinal absorption rate of PFOS was very high, and the adhesion to protein and accumulating in the liver, blood and muscle tissues was probably the main reasons for the biological hazards (Shi et al., 2020). Many studies have found that ng/g levels of PFOS can be detected in aquatic organisms (Lee et al., 2020), terrestrial animals (Dasgupta et al., 2020; Lynch et al., 2019) and related products (Russell et al., 2019; Li et al., 2019), as well as human body (Wang et al., 2019), which posed a potential threat to human health. Therefore, development of simple, rapid and sensitive PFOS detection method is of great importance for PFOS monitoring and environmental health risk appraisal.

As a typical optical signal conduction technique, fluorescence method has the merits of high sensitivity, simplicity, and cost-effective preponderance, which is suitable for the detection of different kinds of trace amounts of analytes (Hessels and Merkx, 2016). Recently, some fluorescent nanomaterials such as fluorescent dyes (Cheng et al., 2018) and carbon quantum dots (CDs) (Chen et al., 2019) have been used to construct fluorescent probes and then were employed for the monitor of PFOS in aqueous samples. However, conventional fluorescent sensors usually suffer from the high-back ground signal, which greatly hinders their detection sensitivity, and few studies were reported on the detection of PFOS using environmentally friendly upconversion fluorescent nanomaterials. As a unique optical nanomaterial, lanthanide-doped upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) can effectively convert near-infrared light into visible light. In comparison with the conventional fluorophores, UCNPs have the advantages of deep penetration, and low background, which are very suitable for application in environmental and biological analysis (Chen et al., 2020, 2019). There are some reports raised upconversion materials to detect typical organic pollutant such as neonicotinoid insecticide acetamiprid (Yang et al., 2019), tetracycline (Yang et al., 2017) and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (Wang et al., 2018) in water samples. Fabrication of the upconversion fluorescent nanoprobe based on NaYF4:Yb3+,Er3+ for detection of PFOS in DMF (N,N-Dimethylformamide) solution has been reported (Li et al., 2019). And in the previous work of our group, upconversion fluorescent nanoprobe toward PFOS was fabricated and implemented for sensitive detection of PFOS in environmental water samples through fluorine-fluorine interaction (Li et al., 2019). For the complex matrix analysis, the selectivity and sensitivity of the surface modification probe to recognize PFOS in aqueous solutions need to be further improved.

The surface molecular imprinting technique is to design the molecular recognition sites on or near the surface of the carrier, so the recognition degree of the template molecules and the utilization rate of the binding sites are boosted (Chen et al., 2016). Recently, researchers have introduced the surface molecular imprinting technique for the construction of fluorescent materials and used as probe for PFOS detection, with satisfactory results obtained in their studies (Feng et al., 2014; Jiao et al., 2018). However, few works were reported on this area.

Herein, we designed a highly sensitive background eliminated surface molecularly imprinting ratiometric fluorescent probe for the detection of PFOS by physically mixing two different UCNPs that emitting green and blue florescence as response signal and reference signal, respectively. Compared to the conventional fluorescence sensor, the probe fabricating strategy offers several unique merits. First, using the environmentally friendly upconversion fluorescent material in the complex samples detection could eliminate the potential interference. Second, introducing the molecularly imprinting technique could improve the detection sensitivity and selectivity greatly. Third, the fabrication strategy of the ratiometric detection avoids the time-consuming synthesis of composite nanoparticles and is easy operation. The developed probe is applied for the PFOS detection in the complex real samples. This work provides a simple, convenient and alternative construction approach to ratiometric fluorescence sensors and which application for the trace pollutant detection in the complex matrixes.

ReCl3·6H2O (Re˭Y, Yb, Tm, Er, Gd, 99.99%), acid (OA, >90%), igepal CO-520, 1-Octadecene (ODE, >90%), PFOS (98%), perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), perfluorohexyl sulfonate potassium (PFHSK), perfluorinated butyl sulfonic acid potassium (PFBSK), perfluoro-1-octanesulfonyl fluoride (PFSF), nonafluorobutane-1-sulfonic acid (OSA), 3-aminopropyl triethoxy silane (APTES), Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 4.6), PBS buffer (pH = 7.0), (N1-((ethylimino)methylene)-N3, N3-dimethy -lpropane-1,3-diamine) (EDC, 98%), N-Hydroxysulfosuccinimide sodium salt (NHS, 98%), were all obtained from Sigma-Aldrich China-Mainland. Perfluoroalkylether potassium sulfonate (F53-B, Purity ≥ 99%) was purchased from Shanghai Synica. Phenol, NH4F, NaOH, cyclohexane, NaCl, acetate, ammonia solution, ethanol, methanol, ethylsilicate (TEOS), N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), carboxyethylsilanetriol sodium salt, (25 wt% solution in H2O), sodium dodecylbenzene sulfonate (SDBS) and hexadecyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), were all received from Xilong Chemical Co.Ltd. The reagents were all of analytical grade and used as receiving. Deionized water is prepared for Mili-Q Direct-Q®5UV water purification system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA, http://www.merckmillipore.com) was used throughout the experiments.

Firstly, according to the improved reference’s method (Qian and Zhang, 2008), two lanthanide-doped upconversion materials excited at 980 nm that NaYF4:Yb3+,Er3+@NaGdF4 (G-UCNPs) and NaYF4:Yb3+,Tm3+@NaGdF4 (B-UCNPs) with emission signals at 478 nm and 546 nm were synthesized, respectively. Then, the G-UCNPs-SiO2 nanocomposite was obtained by combing G-UCNPs with SiO2 nanospheres through amidation reaction (Zhang et al., 2019). Meanwhile, the B-UCNPs@SiO2 nanocomposite with high water dispersity and monodispersity was prepared by coating with silica layer on the surface of B-UCNPs, and which was then used for fabrication the UMIR probe as the analytical reference material (Sun et al., 2018). The detailed preparation conditions for the above processes were shown in Supplementary Material.

Surface imprinted nanopolymers based on G-UCNPs-SiO2 support were then prepared according to the improved reference’s method (Wang et al., 2016). Typically, 20 mg of PFOS was dissolved into 20 mL of ethanol, and then G-UCNPs-SiO2 (20 mg) nanoparticles were evenly dispersed in the above solution under sonication. Subsequently, 0.08 mL APTES, 0.2 mL TEOS and 0.1 mL NH3·H2O were dropped slowly into the above mixture and then stirred at room temperature overnight. The resultant nanoparticles were separated by centrifugation at 8500 rpm, and rinsed with volumes of methanol/acetic acid eluent (9:1, v/v) repeatedly under sonication, until the template molecules were completely removed. Finally, the collected solid products were dried in vacuum at 60 ℃ for 12 h. The final product obtained was denoted as G-UCNPs-SiO2@MIPs, and was employed as the analytical identification material in the UMIR probe. The non-imprinted polymers were synthesized with the same procedures above but in absence of PFOS, which was denoted as G-UCNPs-SiO2@NIPs. The as-prepared G-UCNPs-SiO2@MIPs and G-UCNPs- SiO2@NIPs were dispersed in deioned water, respectively for further use.

All the photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded by fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The microscopy images were observed by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM, FEI TalosF200X field emission Massachusetts, U.S.) and Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM, JSM-6700F JEOL, Japan). The surface functional groups were studied with Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometry (FT-IR, Nicolet-iS5 spectrometry, U.S.) using KBr pellets. The zeta-potentials of G-UCNPs-SiO2@MIPs at pH from 3.0 to 9.0 (adjusted by diluted NaOH or HCl) were determined by Zeta Potential Meter (zeta probe, Colloidal-Dynamics company, America) which using the diluted HCl or NaOH to adjust the pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements of fluorescence response to PFOS
ce:section.ce:para: The aqueous dispersion of the analytical reference material B-UCNPs@SiO2 and identification material G-UCNPs-SiO2@MIPs were mixed in proportional concentrations to form a UMIR probe system. In a typically PFOS detection process, 300 μL of analytical identification material solution (0.015 mg/mL) and 100 μL of reference material solution (0.06 mg/mL) were mixed into a glass vial then certain concentration of PFOS solution dilution by Tris-HCl buffer was injected, and finally using pH = 4.0 of Tris-HCl buffer to volume the test solution into 1.0 mL. The final concentration of the analytical signal material and reference signal material in the system was 4.5 µg/mL and 6.0 µg/mL, respectively. The mixture was then sonicated for 1 min and incubated at room temperature for 10 min before measurement. For the fluorescence test, the excitation was performed using a 980 nm high stability infrared laser (New Industry Optoelectronics Technology Co. LTD, Changchun, China). The excitation and emission slit width were both set as 10 nm, current 210 mA and voltage 900 V. The fluorescence spectra between 200 and 800 nm were recorded, and the fluorescence intensities in 546 nm (I 546) and 478 nm (I 478) were used to calculated the PFOS concentration. As an experimental comparison, the PFOS detection by the corresponding UNIR probe was performed using the same procedure.

The UMIR probe was implemented to detect PFOS in environmental water, human serum and egg extract samples. Water samples were collected from Po-yang Lake (Nanchang, China), human serum was taken from healthy adults (Infirmary of Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang, China) and egg samples were bought from the local market. After the water samples were taken into laboratory, they were filtered by 0.20 µm syringe filter for 3 times and heated to the boiling point for 8–10 min, and then cooled to room temperature naturally. Then appropriate concentration of PFOS was spiked in the samples for use. For the human serum and egg samples (the albumen and yolk were firstly mixed in the laboratory), appropriate amounts of PFOS were spiked into the samples, respectively, and then they were conducted the following extraction and purification procedures. The spiked human serum samples were centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 10 min at room temperature to obtain the supernatant, then same volume of acetonitrile was added to the supernatant and vortexed for 2 min. The mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 r/min to remove the protein from the serum, then filtered by 0.22 µm syringe filter and the filtrate was collected for use. For the spiked egg samples, they were extracted by vortex for 2 min using same volume of acetonitrile with the samples. Then the mixture was centrifuged at 8000 r/min to remove the insoluble substance. After two rounds of extraction, the extracted supernatant was collected and evaporated under mild N2 to dry, and then reconstitute with 10 mL deionized water for the further treatment. The above obtained egg extract samples were following purified by Oasis HLB solid-phase extraction (SPE) according to the improved reference’s method (Wang et al., 2019). The SPE procedure was shown in the Supplementary Material. After the SPE, the collected methanol eluent was evaporated under gentle N2 to dry and reconstitute with 1 mL deionized water, and then filtered with a 0.22 µm syringe filter for use. All the treated samples were stored at 4 ℃ and measured within 24 h. For the water samples analysis, 300 μL of analytical identification material solution (0.015 mg/mL) and 100 μL of reference material solution (0.06 mg/mL) were mixed into a glass vial, and then 100 μL of water sample was added, and finally using pH = 4.0 of Tris-HCl buffer to volume the test solution into 1.0 mL. The mixture was sonicated and incubated and then for fluorescent measurement. For the human serum and egg samples, the obtained treated matrixes were first diluted 50 times with Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 4.0, 1 mM) and used for analysis according to the same procedure.

The surface morphology of the synthesized SiO2 nanospheres, G-UCNPs and G-UCNPs-SiO2@MIPs were shown in Fig. 2. The SEM image in Fig. 2A shows that the SiO2 nanoparticles exhibit highly spherical and monodisperse morphology with an average size of 90–100 nm. And the TEM image in Fig. 2B displays that the G-UCNPs present rectangular crystals with dimensions of 20–30 nm. The TEM images of G-UCNPs-SiO2@MIPs in Fig. 2C indicate that both the upconversion nanoparticles and SiO2 spheres were wrapped in the nano-imprinted layer with a thickness of about 6–10 nm, which also suggest the successful construction of the imprinted layer.

To further ascertain the synthesizing of materials and their surface functional groups, the FT-IR spectra of the three materials including APTES-modified G-UCNPs, G-UCNPs-SiO2 as well as G-UCNPs-SiO2 @MIPs were measured and the results were shown in Fig. 2D. It can be seen that the APTES-modified G-UCNPs emerge a strong and wide Si‒O‒Si antisymmetric stretching peak at 1096 cm−1, aliphatic C‒H stretching band at 2950 cm−1and N‒H band at 3410 (curve a). After the APTES-modified G-UCNPs was combined with carboxylated SiO2 (curve b), the aliphatic C‒H stretching bands at 2950 cm−1 and 3410 cm−1 are shrunk obviously. Meanwhile, the characteristic peaks of amido bond at 1410 cm−1 and 1310 cm−1 were appeared, confirming the combination of these two materials through the amidation reaction. With the modification of surface molecular imprinting layer (curve c), the aliphatic C‒H stretching band at 2950 cm−1 and N‒H band at 3410 cm−1 is enhanced (Tran et al., 2014), suggesting plenty of amine groups existed in the surface of the imprinted materials, which will has great potential to binding with PFOS through electrostatic and H-bond interactions.

In order to get an optimal detection performance to PFOS in real samples, the effects of analytical identification and reference material proportion, pH value of solution, ionic strength, incubation time and temperature on the sensitivity of PFOS detection were investigated in detail. Meanwhile, the fluorescence stability of the probe in aqueous solution was also studied and the results were showed in Supplementary Material.

The response of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing system to PFOS depends on the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 478 nm and 546 nm. We studied the effect of three designed concentration ratios of analytical identification material and reference material that at 3:2, 3:4, 3:6 (C/C, µg/mL:µg/mL), and compared the corresponding sensitivity of detection PFOS by fluorescence quenching efficiency
(
I
546
/
I
478
)
0
/
(
I
546
/
I
478
)
. As illustrated in Fig. 3A–C, when the concentration ratio was set at 3:4, the fluorescence intensities at both wavelengths were very high and comparable (Fig. 3B), and the fitting linear results showed the best detection sensitivity. It hence the content of 4.5 µg/mL of analytical identification material and 6.0 µg/mL of reference material was used in the following experiments.

The solution pH of 3.0–9.0 was examined to evaluate the detection sensitivity. As can be seen in Fig. 4A, the system presents the best detection sensitivity at pH = 4.0. From the synthesizing process and the FT-IR spectrum of the G-UCNPs-SiO2@MIPs, we know that there are a large number of amino groups on the nanomaterials. Under acidic conditions, the amino groups could be protonated and the surface of the material is positively charged, which promotes its interaction with PFOS by forming conjugated acid-base pairs, leading to fluorescence quenching through electrostatic interaction (Feng et al., 2014). The zeta potential results in Fig. 4A showed that the material has maximum positive charge of 22.5 mV at the same pH value, which is very beneficial to the PFOS binding on the probe surface. It's also worth mentioning that in the whole pH range tested from 3.0 to 9.0, the zeta potential values of G-UCNPs-SiO2@MIPs were all above 15 mV, which confirms the existence of amounts of free amino groups in the surface. These results are consistent with the FT-IR results, and also indicates that the G-UCNPs-SiO2@MIPs nanoparticles could keep stable in water solutions rather than tend to flocculate. The wide pH applicability and the high-water stability are very beneficial to the real application of the fluorescent probe for PFOS detection.

We further injected different concentrations of NaCl solution to the sensing system to investigate the effect of ionic strength on PFOS detection. As showed in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the system decreased slightly with the increase of NaCl concentration. It is probably that when salt ions were introduced to the detection system, they competed with the PFOS to bind with the material, and the electrostatic interaction between the sulfonic group of PFOS and the protonated amino group on the probe was weakened, resulting in the fluorescence quenching efficiency decreased. However, only ~10% of quenching efficiency was induced when the concentration of NaCl was high to 0.5 M.

Besides, to investigate the influence of incubation time and temperature in the detection, 0.4 nM PFOS was added to the detection system for fluorescence testing, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded at 478 nm and 546 nm within 5–10 min and 15–60 ℃, respectively. Fig. 4C illustrates that the values of
I
546
/
I
478
decreased slightly as time went on and reached a relatively stable value within 10 min Fig. 4D shows that the detection system was stable when the incubation temperature is 15 ℃–35 ℃. Hence the following experiment was carried out at pH = 4.0 and incubated for 10 min at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of PFOS by the UMIR probe
ce:section.ce:para:
To further evaluate the fluorescence performance of the as-prepared UMIR probe, at the above optimal conditions, we used fluorescence quenching efficiency to evaluate the sensitivity of fluorescent probe for PFOS response. The fluorescence quenching of the UMIR probe follows the Stern-Volmer equation
P
0
/ P = 1 +
K
S V
C
q
, where P 0 and P are the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of PFOS, respectively. C q is the concentration of PFOS and K
SV is the quenching constant of PFOS. The ratio of
K
SV
MIP
to
K
SV
N I P
is defined as the imprinting factor (IF) (Wang et al., 2018).

Fig. 5A and B displays the fluorescence response of UMIR probe and the UNIR probe to PFOS at 546 nm and pH = 4.0. As can be seen from the corresponding linear fitting results in Fig. 5a and b, it shows good linear relationship between fluorescence quenching efficiency and the PFOS concentration, and the related parameters were listed at
Table 2. The K
SV value of the linear regression results indicates that the UMIR probe presented an obvious much higher sensitivity for the PFOS response than that of UNIR probe. The linear range was divided into two segments that of 0.001–0.1 nmol/L (0.5–5 ng/L) and 0.1–1.0 nmol/L (5–500 ng/L), with a limit of detection (LOD) calculated (3σ) of 1 pmol/L (0.5 ng/L). The sensitivity of the developed method is completely meet the requirements that the interim health assessment report of the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) and the European Commission's Water Framework Directive (WFD), which allowing PFOS concentration of 200 ng/L (Environmental Protection Agency EPA, 2014) and 0.65 ng/L (European Commission, Directive, 2013) for drinking water and fresh water, respectively. Comparing with the recently reported results of fluorescence methods that for detection of PFOS listed at Table 1 (Liang et al., 2015), the LOD of this method is at least 100-fold lower than those of using carbon dots, dyes or BSTFA-modified NaYF4:Yb,Er as fluorescent probe, and is comparable to the sensitivity of the carbon dots based molecular imprinted fluorescence method. This suggests the developed method has great superior to the trace detection of PFOS.

In order to investigate the fluorescence performance of the UMIR probe at a mild pH condition, we also studied the response of the fluorescence probe to PFOS at pH = 6.5. The results in Fig. 6 show that the fluorescence quenching efficiency also has a well linear relationship with the PFOS in the concentration of 0.001–0.1 nmol/L and 0.1–1.0 nmol/L, suggesting the applicability of the UMIR probe for PFOS detection in mild pH condition. The calculated linear fitting parameters listed in Table 2 show that the IF values of the UMIR probe at pH = 4.0 were much higher than that at pH = 6.5, especially in the 0.1–1.0 nmol/L PFOS concentration range, indicating a high selectivity of the probe in acidic conditions. This is in consistent with the results of FT-IR and the pH effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective detection of PFOS by the UMIR probe
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the detection selectivity of the UMIR probe toward PFOS, we selected PFOA, PFSF, F-53B, PFBSK, PFHSK, OSA, SDBS, CTAB and phenol as interfering substances to conduct the selective experiments. The standard curve was plotted with the concentration of 0.1–0.9 nmol/L for PFOS and all the substances. The linear fitting results for PFOS and each substance were shown in Fig. 7, and the corresponding K
SV values were listed in Table 3. For all the substances tested, the K
SV values obtained by the UMIR probe were much higher than those by UNIR probe, indicating the high detection sensitivity of the developed UMIR probe. The highest K
SV value of PFOS was attributed to that the imprinted cavities provide more opportunities for target analytes to enter the receptor sites and achieve specific selection for PFOS. The K
SV of PFOA, PFSF and F-53B was much higher than those of other fluoridated compounds and alkyl chain substances, which was probably ascribed to the similar linear C8 molecular structures of these three compounds with PFOS template.

Of particular note is that the established PFOS detection probe also shows a relative high sensitivity and selectivity to PFOA detection, but not to PFSF or F-53B, suggesting both the spatial conformation of the C-F chain skeleton and electrostatic interaction play significant roles in the process of probe recognition and fluorescence response. This sensitivity and selectivity of the probe to PFOA was very comparable to the PFOS, which is agreed well with the results in Feng et al.’s (2014) report. It may be attributed to the synthesized hydrophilic SiO2 imprinting surface was very beneficial to the PFOA binding, which resultant of a higher sensitivity and selectivity. However, for the hydrophobic imprinting polymer layer studied, PFOA exhibited a lower recognition capacity even than the interfering substances those with totally different structures with PFOS (Guo et al., 2018). This result suggested that the hydrophilic interaction probably also play important role in the detection process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application to the real samples analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, many studies have shown that dietary and water intake were the primary routes of animals and human exposure to PFOS. In order to further assess the practical applicability of UMIR probe, the application of constructed nanoprobe for PFOS detection in surface water and complex biological samples has been investigated. All of our tests were enforced on the spiked samples. When the water sample was harvested, the particles were removed and then heated for sterilization. Three different concentrations of PFOS were spiked in the samples and measured using the developed fluorescent nanoprobe, respectively. The human serum samples were thawed at room temperature and then spiked two different concentrations of PFOS, respectively. After mixing thoroughly, they were centrifuged and then removed the protein through precipitating by acetonitrile. The well-mixed egg samples were spiked with two different concentrations of PFOS and then extracted with acetonitrile and following purified with Oasis HLB SPE column. All the above extracted biological samples were filtered with 0.22 µm syringe filter and then diluted 50 times with Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 4.0, 1 mM) for the analysis use. The corresponding sensing detection results for all the spiking samples were shown in Table 4. It can be seen from the recovery rates results that the UMIR probe presents well accuracy and repeatability for PFOS detection in these spiking matrixes, with recovery rates of 70.6–106.0% and relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.27–8.8% obtained.

The results above indicated that the developed UMIR probe could be used in quantitative detection of PFOS in the complex biological sample matrix well, and considering of the superiority of the upconversion fluorescence nanoparticles, it is expected to with potential application in the in vivo analysis and bioimaging to C-8 series of perfluorinated compounds. However, as a proof-of-concept method and like what we reported in our previous work (Lin et al., 2019), the developed method might still not proper for endogenous content detection of PFOS in real samples. If we wanted to applied it to the practical use for the endogenous PFOS determination, more efficient pretreatment method should be developed and that is exactly what we have been focusing on in our recent work.

A surface molecular imprinting ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe was constructed by physically mixing the responsive signal and reference signal materials for efficient detection of PFOS. The constructed UMIR probe exhibited excellent fluorescence response to PFOS through the imprinted recognition effect and PET effect. The developed strategy exhibits a low detection limit of 1 pmol/L for PFOS, which is 102–104 times lower than the most fluorescence probes reported recently. High specificity to the template molecules were achieved with two segments linear ranges of 0.001–0.1 nmol/L and 0.1–1.0 nmol/L, respectively. Moreover, the constructed probe was suitable for the determination of PFOS in environmental water and complex biological samples matrix. We expect this strategy would furnish the potential application of the proposed UMIR probe in the realm of environmental and biological monitoring.

Lingxi Tian: Data curation, Writing - original draft preparation. Huiqin Guo: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Jing Li: Methodology. Liushui Yan: Supervision. Enze Zhu: Validation; Xiaoming Liu: Visualization; Kexin Li: Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21665018, 21966023 and 51568049); Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province of China (No. 20181BAB203014). We would like to appreciate Prof. Wen-Qing Tu from Research Institute of Poyang Lake, Jiangxi Academy of Science for the providing of Po-yang Lake water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2021.125353.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
